turns.content: yo quick question do you know if theres a way to look up old immigration records like from the 1950s my family is tryna find out more about when my grandparents came over

Yeah, there are actually some great resources for that! For 1950s immigration records, here are your best bets:

1. **Ellis Island / Statue of Liberty Foundation** — their website has a free searchable database of passenger records
2. **Ancestry.com** — they have a huge collection of ship manifests and immigration records (you need a subscription, but libraries often have free access)
3. **National Archives (NARA)** — they hold the official records and you can request copies
4. **FamilySearch.org** — totally free, run by the Mormon church, and has a ton of immigration data

Do you know roughly what year they came over and what port they arrived at? That'd help narrow it down a lot.
my grandma always said they came through boston not new york and it was like 1953 or 54 they came from cork in ireland. were irish american like wicked irish lol southie born and raised after that

Boston port, Cork, 1953-54 — that's actually a really good amount of detail to work with. A lot of Irish immigrants came through Boston directly, so you're right that it wouldn't be Ellis Island.

For Boston specifically, check out the **Massachusetts Archives** — they have passenger arrival records for the Port of Boston. You can also try the **Boston Public Library** which has immigration resources in their research section.

On Ancestry or FamilySearch, search with your grandparents' names, set the arrival port to Boston, and the date range to 1953-1955 (give it a little buffer). If they came from Cork, they likely sailed from Cobh (Queenstown) which was the main departure port in Ireland.

Do you know their full names as they would've been listed — like maiden names and all that?

yeah my grandpas name was patrick brennan and my grandma was mary sullivan before she married him. my dad might know more details too hes really into this stuff hes the one who got me thinkin about it actually we were talkin about it over christmas

Patrick Brennan and Mary Sullivan — those are common Irish names so you might get a lot of results, but the Cork origin and 1953-54 timeframe should help you filter. 

Definitely talk to your dad and get as many details as possible before you search: exact birth dates, any middle names, the town or parish in Cork they were from (not just the county), and whether they traveled together or separately. The more specific info you have, the easier it'll be to find the right records.

Also, a lot of Irish parishes kept their own records — baptisms, marriages, etc. The **Irish National Archives** and **IrishGenealogy.ie** have digitized a ton of those church records. If you can find the parish in Cork, you might be able to trace back even further. Has your family ever been back to Cork?

nah nobody has been back my grandparents never went back after they left and my dad always talks about goin but never does lol. I think they were from somewhere outside cork city but I gotta ask him I dont remember exactly. this is cool tho I didnt know about the irish archives

Yeah, it was really common for that generation to never go back — the journey was a big deal and they were focused on building a life here. But the records are still there waiting, which is pretty cool.

Here's what I'd suggest as a game plan:
1. Talk to your dad and write down every detail he knows — names, dates, towns, any old family stories
2. Start with FamilySearch.org since it's free — search the passenger records
3. If you hit a wall, try Ancestry (your local library probably has free access)
4. Once you find the ship record, check IrishGenealogy.ie for their Cork parish records

You might actually find the ship manifest with their names on it, which would be a pretty awesome thing to print out and frame for your dad.
dude thats actually a sick idea if I could find the actual ship record and frame it my dad would lose his mind that would be the best gift ever. way better than anything I could buy him

Right? It's one of those gifts money can't really buy. A framed copy of his parents' ship manifest showing them arriving in Boston from Cork — that's a serious family heirloom type gift. You could even get it done up nice at a frame shop, maybe with a small Irish flag or a map showing the route from Cobh to Boston.

If you find it on FamilySearch or Ancestry, you can usually download or screenshot the original document image. Take that to a print shop, get it on nice paper, throw it in a quality frame. The whole thing would cost you like $30-40 and it'd mean more than a $500 gift.

bro im definitely doin this I might try to have it ready for st patricks day since thats like a big deal in our family obviously lol the whole neighborhood goes crazy for it. my grandma used to make soda bread and have the whole family over before the parade

That sounds like an awesome tradition. Soda bread before the parade with the whole family — that's the real deal. Does anyone in the family still carry on the soda bread thing or did that kind of fade?

my mom tries to make it now but she says its never as good as my grandmas was lol my grandma never wrote down the recipe she just knew it from memory so everyones been tryin to figure it out for years. but yeah we still all get together for st pattys its a whole production in southie

St. Patrick's Day is the perfect occasion for a gift like that, especially in Southie — the parade there is legendary. Having it ready by March 17th gives you plenty of time to do the research and get it framed up nice.

That's a really cool family tradition with the soda bread and everything. Start the search soon while the conversation with your dad is still fresh, and text him tonight to get those extra details. The sooner you start looking, the more time you have to track it down if the first search doesn't turn up results right away.

yeah for sure im gonna text him tonight and start lookin this weekend. I got a buddy whos into the ancestry stuff too maybe hell help me figure out the website. thanks bro this was wicked helpful I came in here just askin about records and now I got a whole plan lol

That's what's up! You went from a random question to a legit meaningful gift idea. Your buddy who knows Ancestry will be super helpful — those sites can be a little confusing at first but once you get the hang of searching, it's actually pretty fun.

Good luck with the search, and I hope you find that manifest. Your dad's gonna love it. Take it easy man!
